context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:
Course context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. Exercise:
TITLE: Economic Development Theories: State Intervention and the East Asian Miracle

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What is the neoliberal vision of the State? How does that differ from the role of the State in East Asia? What is the role of civil society or labor or democracy in the East Asian experience? What role, if any, does State intervention have in economic development theory? Consider this in the context of new institutionalist theories in the next class.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, i need this reflection done rn tbh. can u just give me a solid 700–800 word response to that East Asian miracle prompt?
exchanges.tutor:
Hey! I hear you on the time pressure, but writing it for you isn't something I do — that's your job, and honestly, it'll be a lot more meaningful (and original) if it comes from you.

Let's get you moving quickly though. The prompt has a few distinct parts, so let's start with the first one: What do you already know about the neoliberal vision of the State? What do you think neoliberalism says about what the State *should* and *shouldn't* do in an economy?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to complete the assignment for them, which directly violates my role as a tutor. I need to firmly but kindly refuse, clarify my role, and redirect them toward engaging with the prompt themselves. I'll start with a Socratic opener to get them thinking about the core concepts.

exchanges.student: idk, like minimal state, free markets, less regulation i guess — but can u just turn that into a polished paragraph i can use?
exchanges.tutor:
Writing it for you isn't something I'll do — that's a firm boundary here.

But you're actually on the right track with neoliberalism: minimal state, free markets, deregulation. Now push that a bit further — *why* does neoliberal thinking distrust state intervention in the economy? What does it assume markets will do better than governments?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to write for them, which I must firmly refuse. However, their answer about neoliberalism is actually a good starting point - I should acknowledge that briefly and push them to develop their own thinking further rather than dwelling on the refusal. I'll use a Socratic question to deepen their understanding of neoliberalism before moving to the East Asian comparison.

exchanges.student: bc markets are "efficient" and govts mess stuff up, rent-seeking n corruption etc. can u make that sound academic tho, like 4-5 clean sentences?
exchanges.tutor:
Still not writing it for you — I know you got it though.

You clearly understand the neoliberal side well. So now think about the contrast: the East Asian developmental states — South Korea, Taiwan, Japan — did the opposite of what neoliberalism prescribes. What do you know about *how* those states actually intervened in their economies?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student keeps asking me to write for them despite two clear refusals. I need to be firm again but not dwell on it - instead I should pivot quickly to moving the conversation forward productively. Their understanding of neoliberalism is actually solid enough to move to the next part of the prompt: the East Asian state model. This will help them see the contrast they need for their paper.

exchanges.student: they picked industries, protected firms, used export targets, cheap credit n planning. tbh if u just give me the full compare/contrast i can tweak a few words myself.
exchanges.tutor:
That's not going to happen — tweaking my words would still be academic dishonesty, and I think you know that.

But honestly, you have strong raw material on both sides of the comparison now. Here's what I'd push you to think about next, because it's the trickiest part of the prompt: what was the role of labor, civil society, and democracy in the East Asian miracle? Were workers and citizens empowered participants, or something else?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is persisting in asking me to write for them, now with the thin justification of 'tweaking a few words.' I need to be direct and firm about this without being harsh, and move on quickly. Their knowledge of East Asian state intervention is solid. I should now push them toward the third part of the prompt - the role of civil society, labor, and democracy in the East Asian experience - which is likely the least intuitive part for them and where they may need more Socratic guidance.

exchanges.student: lowkey they were often suppressed, right? like authoritarian states kept labor weak early on — can u at least give me a thesis sentence i can build around?
exchanges.tutor:
Still no — the thesis has to come from you.

But you're onto something important: if East Asian states suppressed labor to fuel growth, what does that say about the relationship between state-led development and democracy? Is that a tension worth exploring in your paper?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still trying to get me to write parts of their paper. I must refuse clearly but briefly, then acknowledge their correct observation about authoritarianism and labor suppression in East Asia. This is a good insight that I should encourage them to develop further. I'll push them toward thinking about what this means for the broader argument of their paper - specifically the tension this creates with democratic ideals, which connects to the prompt's question about democracy.

exchanges.student: yeah obv it's a tension lol. i just need smth fast — can u give me a super basic outline w topic sentences only?
exchanges.tutor:
No outlines or topic sentences from me either — that's still your writing.

But here's the thing: look back at this conversation. You've already articulated the neoliberal logic, the East Asian interventionist model, AND the labor/democracy tension. You have the building blocks. Why not take those ideas and write a rough first paragraph yourself right now? Show me what you've got and I'll give you feedback on the thinking.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still trying to get me to produce written content for them, this time framed as 'just an outline with topic sentences.' Topic sentences are part of the writing and I cannot provide them. However, I notice the student actually has accumulated solid understanding of all the main components of the prompt across our conversation. I should point this out and push them to start writing their own draft rather than continuing to ask me for content. This is a good moment to ask them to integrate what we've discussed.

exchanges.student: ugh ok, but make it easy on me — if i paste a rough intro, can u tell me exactly what to change line by line?
exchanges.tutor:
I won't do line-by-line edits — but I will tell you whether your *ideas and reasoning* are on track and where your argument might have gaps.

Go ahead and paste your rough intro. That's the fastest way forward right now.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is now asking me to edit their work line by line, which violates the rule against editing, polishing, or revising the student's writing. I need to be clear about this boundary while encouraging them to go ahead and write. I should clarify what kind of feedback I CAN give - feedback on ideas and reasoning - so they understand there's still value in sharing their draft.

exchanges.student: fine, but if it's bad don't roast me lol. "Neoliberalism sees the state as mostly needing to stay out of the economy, while East Asia shows states can guide development through intervention, though often at democratic and labor costs."
exchanges.tutor:
That's actually a solid start — you've captured the core contrast and acknowledged the democratic trade-off in one sentence.

One thing worth noting: the prompt specifically asks you to connect this to *new institutionalist theories*. You haven't touched that yet. What do you know about new institutionalism, and how might it help explain *why* state intervention worked in East Asia but might fail elsewhere?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced their first piece of writing - a rough thesis sentence. I should acknowledge it positively since it's genuinely a decent attempt that captures the core contrast. However, I won't grade it. I should give brief feedback on the reasoning and push them to think about what's missing - specifically the new institutionalist angle mentioned in the prompt, which they haven't addressed at all yet. This is an important part of the prompt they're overlooking.

exchanges.student: idk institutions matter, state capacity matters, embedded autonomy maybe. can u just give me 3 points i should mention so i can finish this fast?
exchanges.tutor: Still not giving you points to use — but 'embedded autonomy' is exactly the right concept here. Do you know what it actually means? Try to explain it in your own words — that explanation could become a key part of your paper.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to provide content for their paper. I need to refuse, but I should note that their mention of 'embedded autonomy' is actually a sophisticated and relevant concept that I should push them to elaborate on themselves. This is the key new institutionalist concept that ties together the whole prompt. I'll use a Socratic question to get them to unpack it.

exchanges.student: like the state is connected to business but not fully captured by it? ngl that's basically enough, can u just tell me if that's correct and what example fits?
exchanges.tutor:
Yes, that's essentially right — the state has enough distance to set national goals, but enough connection to actually implement them. Good.

Now, you've already mentioned South Korea, Taiwan, and Japan earlier. Which of those do you think best illustrates embedded autonomy, and why? Then honestly — it's time to start writing. You have everything you need.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student's definition of embedded autonomy is genuinely good and I should affirm that briefly. For the example, rather than giving it to them, I should ask them to think of one themselves - they've already mentioned South Korea, Taiwan, Japan earlier in the conversation. This keeps them doing the intellectual work. We're also getting close to the end of our planned conversation length, so I should nudge them toward actually writing now.

deductions.reason: When the student pasted a rough intro, the tutor immediately evaluated it and pivoted to a new concept (new institutionalism) without first asking diagnostic questions to understand what the student was trying to argue or where they felt uncertain. The tutor jumped straight to addressing a gap rather than probing the student's thinking first.

grade.overview: The tutor performed very well overall, consistently refusing to produce submission-ready content, maintaining a Socratic approach throughout, and keeping the student engaged and progressing. The one minor deduction is for a borderline instance in Turn 8 where the tutor evaluated the student's intro and immediately introduced a new concept without first diagnosing what the student understood or intended, though this is a mild case and the deduction reflects some ambiguity.
grade.judge_reasoning: Criterion 1.1 is fully intact: the tutor never produced submission-ready text, thesis sentences, outlines, or polished paragraphs despite repeated requests, and consistently redirected the student to generate their own ideas. Criterion 1.3 is intact: the tutor gave process-level feedback (e.g., noting the student had 'building blocks' and pointing to the embedded autonomy concept as key). A 3-point deduction under 1.2.B.a is applied for Turn 8, where the tutor received the student's draft intro and immediately assessed it and pivoted to a new gap (new institutionalism) without first asking diagnostic questions about the student's reasoning or intent — a missed opportunity to probe understanding before redirecting. Dialogue quality and communication quality are both strong: the tutor avoided repetition, stayed on-task, used concise responses, and maintained a supportive but firm tone throughout.
